narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, your background and academic record were carefully evaluated by an admissions model designed to predict first-time success on the bar exam, using data from thousands of past students. The model assessed factors like your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, year-in-school grade deciles, family income, and whether you'd study full-time. It calculated a 58% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank of around 5.5; this stood out as the strongest signal to the model that your later academic performance might not translate to bar success. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 34, below the average of 36.7, which pulled somewhat in the opposite direction by suggesting potential for passing, though not enough to offset other concerns. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, also under the typical 3.2 average, provided a milder positive nudge toward passing but couldn't overcome the dominant negative influence. While your upper-middle family income and full-time student status (shared by over 90% of applicants) played smaller roles, they didn't sway the overall outlook. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows stronger early grades (Year 1 decile of 3 versus the average 5.7) but a concerning drop-off later, which the model flagged as a key risk not seen as often in successful candidates. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—many from similar backgrounds thrive with targeted preparation—but the model saw these patterns as higher-risk based on historical data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your resilience and any bar prep plans. Schools value persistence, and addressing these core areas could flip the prediction in your favor.
